messages.text: What is the status/results of any 3rd party testing done on Rejuv vs competition (with asphalt shingles)?
<@U06F97BPQJU> This was the work that CB paid for. Note that SoyLei A, B, and C are (going with your Rejuv naming convention here) Rejuv, Rejuv-Polymer, and RoofMaxx.

attachments.name: SOYL 01-02-01.03.T&E  FInal  Report Prototype Shingle Rejuvenators + Appendix Photos. 7.07.2021.pdf

messages.text: I think Baker is taking the lead and synthesizing this into a paper for submittal soon. Once in a paper and submitted, it is considered public.
Great, now who can distill the results down to a "marketing type" single page handout that could be given to someone of interest at a show or conference? And how long would that take?
I can probably get you something by monday or Tuesday.
The fire resistance is very interesting...could be a seperate product that has broader application.

WRT the shingle application, we've talked about that from the insurance angle in the past, but I don't know that anyone ever strongly pursued it. WRT future applications, I think the obvious answer is are there places where vegetable oils, waxes, or other similar long chain waxy compounds are used, where fire is a concern?
There is also this report which contains experiments conducted at ISU

Clearly it’s not a fire retardant on its own…but its secondary attribute as more of a fire suppressant, that’s non toxic, could be key for say a cedar shingles, as an example…retardant chems are nasty… more work and more thought

Do we know a lab that test materials as retardants?
I am not certain PRI does this type of testing. Also may want to evaluate clay roofing tiles.
Found this lab with a quick search. I will ask for a quote to see how much it costs.

<@U06F97BPQJU> A more general question for you, somewhat related to the above discussion: what do you think our level of interest on derivative molecules of the Rejuv platform?Expanding on the idea talked about in the <#C06F7SD9Z0A> channel, one of the ideas is intentionally reaction the epoxy with something to change properties. This can be things like organic acids, epoxy curatives (amines/thiols), etc. We've had concerns about that causing regulatory hurdles associated with that approach and I'm hoping you have some thoughts.

To the flame retardant idea, if we wanted to run with it and combine compatibility with shingles and similar material with flame retardants, we could look at grafting actual flame retardants onto the epoxies. For something like that we would likely be able to use commodity ESO, rather than SESO, which is a lot more commercially available. For example, throw ESO/SESO in a pot with something like chlorendic acid and cook it for a bit and you'll have a flame-retardant veg-oil.

Chlorendic acid is probably not the best choice because of politics of halogenated flame retardants, but I used it as an example because it's the first thing that came up and has the necessary functional groups. If this idea is of interest, we can expand it out a bit, but I'd like to hear your thoughts on regulations first.
